---
title: "{{< var md-01.title >}}"
subtitle: "{{< var course.long-title >}}"
author:
  - name: "{{< var author >}}"
    affiliations:
        - name: Global Health Engineering - ETH Zurich
          url: https://ghe.ethz.ch/
date: "{{< var course.start-date >}}"
date-format: long
footer: "[{{< fa link >}} {{< var course.site-short >}}]({{< var course.site >}})"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: ../style/slides.scss
    transition: fade
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
editor: source
execute:
  freeze: auto
  echo: true
bibliography: ../references.bib
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(countdown)
```

# B1: Welcome, concept-map intro, baseline map {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Meet the lecturer {.smaller}

**{{< var author >}}**

![](images/lars_schoebitz_profile_photo.jpg){fig-alt="Headshot of Lars Schöbitz" fig-align="left" width="50%"}

-   Environmental Engineer {{< fa hammer >}}
-   Retired researcher {{< fa bed >}}
-   [RStudio certified instructor](https://education.rstudio.com/trainers/)
-   [Data steward at ETHZ](https://ghe.ethz.ch/ghe-blog-news/2024/02/blog-attention-prof-you-need-a-data-steward-for-your-team.html)

## What this day is, and is not

::: incremental
-   Today [is]{.highlight-yellow} the Git and GitHub foundation for collaboration: create, clone, commit, push, branch, pull request, review, merge.
-   Today is [not]{.highlight-yellow} an AI workshop. Working with AI coding agents builds on exactly these skills; that is the follow-up workshop.
-   It is [safe to fail]{.highlight-yellow} here. Everything we do today is reversible, and breaking things is part of the plan.
:::

::: {.notes}
Defuse the AI-agent expectation gap now, in minute 5, not in minute 90. Say the safe-to-fail promise out loud; it comes back in B3 with the revert demo.
:::

## Learning objectives

By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

::: incremental
1.  [Create]{.highlight-yellow} and [clone]{.highlight-yellow} a repository, and run the full [pull, stage, commit, push]{.highlight-yellow} cycle from the RStudio IDE, including reverting a commit.

2.  Work with [branches]{.highlight-yellow} and [pull requests]{.highlight-yellow}: create a branch, open a pull request, [review]{.highlight-yellow} a colleague's pull request, and [merge]{.highlight-yellow} after review.

3.  [Publish]{.highlight-yellow} a rendered document via GitHub Pages, and draw a [concept map]{.highlight-yellow} of how Git and GitHub fit together.
:::

## Teaching strategy

-   Teaching Git on a "need to know" basis
-   Teaching only a very small subset of Git's functionality
-   Creating a safe space for learning and getting support (use Element)
-   Focus on collaboration aspects of GitHub
-   Rhythm in every block: short slides, my turn (watch), your turn (do)

## Schedule

```{r}
#| tbl-colwidths: [25,75]
#| echo: false

read_csv(here::here("data/tbl-01-gitforsci-ghe-course-schedule.csv")) |>
  filter(day == 1) |>
  select(Time = time, Module = title) |>
  kable()

```

##  {background-image="images/github_friends.png"}

::: footer
Illustrations from the [Openscapes](https://openscapes.org/) blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes
:::

::: {.notes}
Why GitHub?

"Collaboration is the most compelling reason to manage a project with Git and GitHub. My definition of collaboration includes hands-on participation by multiple people, including your past and future self, as well as an asymmetric model, in which some people are active makers and others only read or review." - Jenny Bryan

Bryan J. 2017. Excuse me, do you have a moment to talk about version control? PeerJ Preprints 5:e3159v2 https://doi.org/10.7287/peerj.preprints.3159v2
:::

##  {background-image="images/github_fall.png"}

::: footer
Illustrations from the [Openscapes](https://openscapes.org/) blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes
:::

::: {.notes}
GitHub helps us fail safely. Because we can return to previous versions easily, painful consequences of failing are reduced. We can bravely try things knowing that some will fail, and that failing is not catastrophic: we start again from where we left off, with a clear history to learn from.

This is the safe-to-fail promise from the expectations slide, now with a picture.
:::

##  {background-image="images/github_compare_text.png"}

::: footer
Illustrations from the [Openscapes](https://openscapes.org/) blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes
:::

::: {.notes}
GitHub streamlines our work: it takes care of otherwise time-consuming bookkeeping and unrelenting file tracking. Instead of fretting over whether "analysis_final_v2.xls" or "analysis_final_final.xls" is truly the final version, we can be confident in the completed analysis and move on. This connects directly to what learners draw in the baseline map in a few minutes: email attachments and file naming.
:::

##  {background-image="images/github_harness.jpeg"}

::: footer
Illustrations from the [Openscapes](https://openscapes.org/) blog GitHub for supporting, contributing, and failing safely by Allison Horst and Julia Lowndes
:::

::: {.notes}
Once we are empowered to work collaboratively with GitHub, a whole new world of projects opens up, because we can contribute in many different ways. This transferrable ability to collaborate might just be the best thing about GitHub. Keep this to one breath; the motivation section ends here at about minute 10.
:::

## Concept maps {.smaller}

A concept map is [things]{.highlight-yellow} (nodes) joined by [labelled arrows]{.highlight-yellow}, where every label is a verb.

![](images/concept-map-exemplar.png){width="70%" fig-alt="A concept map about making tea. Five oval nodes labelled kettle, water, tea leaves, cup, and tea are joined by arrows labelled with capitalised verbs: HEATS, STEEPS, BECOME, MAKES, and HOLDS."}

Read an arrow out loud and it becomes a sentence: "the kettle [HEATS]{.highlight-yellow} the water."

::: {.notes}
Per Greg Wilson, Teaching Tech Together: concepts as nodes, labelled relationships as edges. Not a flowchart, not a timeline, not a list; no start, no end.

One sentence on why: experts use concept maps to make their mental models visible. Yours today will show how you currently think documents travel, and this evening the second map will show what changed.

8-10 minutes for this intro including the exemplar walk-through.
:::

## Your turn: your baseline map {.smaller}

:::: task
[How does a document you write reach your co-author and come back? Draw it as a concept map.]{.highlight-yellow}

-   Things as nodes, arrows with verb labels.
-   There is no wrong map; this one is yours.
-   Keep the map. You will need it at 14:15.

::: hand
Place the yellow sticky note on your laptop when your map is done.
:::
::::

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 8)
```

::: {.notes}
One single prompt, not a fused double question. Expected content: email attachments, Dropbox, Google Docs, file naming (final_v2, final_final). That is the point; the day connects Git to these habits. Do not correct anything here.

Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at the next break.
:::

# B2: Create and clone (website repo) {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Live drawing: local and remote

[Projector goes to sleep.]{.highlight-yellow} We draw this one by hand.

::: {.notes}
Live drawing 1 (local and remote): two boxes, clone first, then push and pull. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: the export in the appendix of the handbook, images/drawing-1-local-remote.png.
:::

## My turn

<!-- Filled by issue #14: create and clone demo script -->

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #14: learners create and clone their website repo -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 10)
```

::: {.notes}
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at the next break.
:::

## Take a break

[Please get up and move!]{.highlight-yellow} Leave your emails alone. We continue at 11:15.

![](images/pixel-art-tree.png){width="50%" fig-alt="Pixel art of a small character resting under a large leafy tree on a green hill, next to a gentle stream under a clear blue sky."}

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 15)
```

::: footer
Image generated with [DALL-E 3 by OpenAI](https://openai.com/blog/dall-e/)
:::

# B3: The commit cycle (website repo) {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Live drawing: the commit cycle

[Projector goes to sleep.]{.highlight-yellow} We draw this one by hand.

::: {.notes}
Live drawing 2 (commit cycle): working files, stage, commit as labelled snapshot on the history line, push. Leave room for the history line to grow. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-2-commit-cycle.png.
:::

## My turn

<!-- Filled by issue #15: commit cycle, revert demo, credentials moment -->

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #15: learners run the full pull-stage-commit-push cycle -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 10)
```

::: {.notes}
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at the next break.
:::

# B4: Collaboration cliffhanger {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Slides

<!-- Filled by issue #16: pairing slide -->

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #16: clone the partner's repo, hit the push wall -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 10)
```

::: {.notes}
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair; 1:1 fix at lunch.
:::

## Lunch

<!-- Filled by issue #16: debrief and lunch slide with the hard 12:55 return -->

# B5: Branch (manuscript repo) {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Live drawing: branching

[Projector goes to sleep.]{.highlight-yellow} We draw this one by hand.

::: {.notes}
Live drawing 3 (branching): the main line splits, commits land on the branch, the merge point stays empty until drawing 4. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-3-branching.png.
:::

## My turn

<!-- Filled by issue #17: branch demo on the manuscript repo -->

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #17: learners branch on their own manuscript repo -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 10)
```

::: {.notes}
Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair. Regroup check before B6: is your branch pushed? Thumbs up.
:::

# B6: Pull request, review, merge {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Live drawing: the pull request

[Projector goes to sleep.]{.highlight-yellow} We draw this one by hand.

::: {.notes}
Live drawing 4 (pull request): complete the merge point from drawing 3, pause at the join for the human review, then the lines meet. Under 3 minutes. Fallback: images/drawing-4-pull-request.png.
:::

## My turn

<!-- Filled by issue #18: PR, review, merge demo -->

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #18: learners open, review, and merge PRs in pairs -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 15)
```

::: {.notes}
This block is protected. If the day is behind, B7 Publish is cut, never this. Bounded regroup: 2 minutes, then park-and-pair.
:::

# B7: Publish {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Our turn

<!-- Filled by issue #19: publish via GitHub Pages together -->

::: {.notes}
First block to cut. If running behind at 14:05, skip to the fallback slide and hand the time to B8.
:::

## Fallback: what publishing looks like

<!-- Filled by issue #19: pre-published example, steps in one breath -->

::: {.notes}
This slide stands alone for the cut case.
:::

# B8: Concept map revisit and wrap-up {background-color="#4C326A"}

## Your turn

<!-- Filled by issue #20: closing concept map, compare with the morning map -->

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown(minutes = 8)
```

::: {.notes}
Hard start at 14:15: whatever block is running stops. B8 is protected and runs even on the worst day.
:::

## Wrap-up

<!-- Filled by issue #20: recap, side benefits, follow-up, feedback -->

## Thanks!

Slides created via revealjs and Quarto: <https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs/>

Access slides as [PDF on GitHub](https://github.com/gitforsci-ghe/website/raw/main/workshop/slides.pdf)

All material is licensed under [Creative Commons Attribution Share Alike 4.0 International](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/).

## References
